coredata.dc:title: Novel thermotropic system with UV-blocking ability for dynamic radiative cooling and synchronous visible/NIR tuning of windows in buildings in hot climate regions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Science: Advanced Materials and Devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Nunes, Paulo Joaquim
Fernandes, Mariana
de Zea Bermudez, Verónica
coredata.dc:description: Passive dynamic thermotropic (TT) radiative cooling (RC) windows aid net-zero energy buildings in smart cities by adjusting sunlight and solar heat in response to temperature variations, as they become opaque when temperatures rise, thereby using thermal radiation to dissipate heat into outer space. They are ideal for controlling solar heat gain and privacy. Here, we introduce a innovative eco-friendly TT layer with ultraviolet (UV)-shielding and RC ability composed of a sol-gel derived di-ureasil hybrid matrix doped with carbon dots (CDs) obtained from Agapanthus africanus leaves and variable contents of TT 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([BMIm]Cl) ionic liquid. The layers exhibit high transparency and thermal stability. The thermotropic devices (TTDs) assembled demonstrated, at the highest [BMIm]Cl concentration used, improved thermo-optical performance mediated by the surface plasmon resonance effect (SPRE), broad operational range (30–70 °C), and excellent cycling stability. Maximum transmittance variation (ΔT) values of 31/27 % at 555/1100 nm were achieved. This new class of TT layer represents a milestone in the development of smart RC materials for autonomous solar modulation windows.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Passive solar energy management
Zero-energy buildings
Thermotropic windows
di-ureasil hybrid layer
Agapanthus africanus (L.) Hoffmann leaves-based carbon dots
[BMIm]Cl
Ag-mediated surface plasmon resonance effect

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding by SOLPOWINS \u2013 Solar-Powered Smart Windows for Sustainable Buildings (PTDC/CTM-REF/4304/2020)) project, financed by FCT and FEDER, NORTE2020, and CQ-VR (CQVR-UIDB/0616/2020 and CQVR-UIDP/0616/2020) is acknowledged. The work was developed under project A-MoVeR \u2013 \u201CMobilizing Agenda for the Development of Products; Systems towards an Intelligent and Green Mobility\u201D, operation 02/C05-i01.01/2022. PC646908627-00000069, approved under the terms of the call n.\u00B0 02/C05-i01/2022 \u2013 Mobilizing Agendas for Business Innovation, financed by European funds provided to Portugal by the Recovery and Resilience Plan, in the scope of the European Recovery and Resilience Facility, framed in the Next Generation UE, for the period from 2021 to 2026. P.J. Nunes acknowledges CQ-VR/FCT for a grant (https://doi.org/10.54499/UI/BD/151084/2021). M. Fernandes thanks FCT-UTAD for the contract in the scope of DL 57/2016\u2013Lei 57/2017 (https://doi.org/10.54499/DL57/2016/CP1378/CT0001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the TTD prototype based on the d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 hybrid layer, and functionalized glasses decorated with Ag islands. (b) SPRE effect. (c) AFM image of the glass/Ag substrate. (d) Operation of a passive switchable TTW for solar control fabricated with the di-ureasil POP/silica layer doped with CZ-300-3 CDs and [BMIm]Cl.
(a) TGA curves, (b) DSC curves, and (c) XRD patterns of the d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 layers with n = 50 (red line), n = 10 (green line), and n = 5 (blue line).
3D topographical AFM images of the d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 layers with n = 50 (a), n = 10 (b), and n = 5 (c).

(a) Contact angle of water drops deposited on the d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 layers' surface. The dashed line represents the threshold for hydrophobic surfaces (contact angle values higher than 90°). (b) Surface free energy (SFE) of the hybrids. Bars are divided into polar (red) and dispersive (blue) components.
Wetting envelopes of the d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 layers with n = 50 (a), n = 10 (b) and n = 5 (c).

Performance of the Ag-TTDdU'n prototypes. Temperature dependence of the transmission spectra (left column: a, b, c) for n = 50 (top), n = 10 (middle), and n = 5 (bottom) between 30 and 70 °C; Visible photographs of the prototypes (d, e, and f, respectively); Transmittance change in the vis and NIR regions (g, see Table S1 in the Supplementary Material).
Cycling stability (left column: a, b, c) of the Ag-TTDdU'n prototypes with n = 50 (top), n = 10 (middle), and n = 5 (bottom) at 30 and 70 °C and LWIR images (right column: d, e, f).

Transmittance spectra of the Ag-TTDdU′5 prototype in the solar region at 30 °C (black line) and 70 °C (red line) and transmittance spectrum of the d-U′(2000)5[BMIm]Cl film in the thermal region. The orange shaded area represents the AM1.5G photon flux recorded with a solar simulator (model 10500, Abet Technologies). The vertical blue bar represents the atmospheric transparency window.

head.ce:title: Novel thermotropic system with UV-blocking ability for dynamic radiative cooling and synchronous visible/NIR tuning of windows in buildings in hot climate regions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CQ-VR, University of Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (UTAD), 5000-801, Vila Real, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (UTAD)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CQ-VR, University of Tras-os-Montes e Alto Douro (UTAD), 5000-801 Vila Real, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (UTAD), 5000-801, Vila Real, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (UTAD)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Tras-os-Montes e Alto Douro (UTAD), 5000-801 Vila Real, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Passive dynamic thermotropic (TT) radiative cooling (RC) windows aid net-zero energy buildings in smart cities by adjusting sunlight and solar heat in response to temperature variations, as they become opaque when temperatures rise, thereby using thermal radiation to dissipate heat into outer space. They are ideal for controlling solar heat gain and privacy. Here, we introduce a innovative eco-friendly TT layer with ultraviolet (UV)-shielding and RC ability composed of a sol-gel derived di-ureasil hybrid matrix doped with carbon dots (CDs) obtained from Agapanthus africanus leaves and variable contents of TT 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([BMIm]Cl) ionic liquid. The layers exhibit high transparency and thermal stability. The thermotropic devices (TTDs) assembled demonstrated, at the highest [BMIm]Cl concentration used, improved thermo-optical performance mediated by the surface plasmon resonance effect (SPRE), broad operational range (30–70 °C), and excellent cycling stability. Maximum transmittance variation (ΔT) values of 31/27 % at 555/1100 nm were achieved. This new class of TT layer represents a milestone in the development of smart RC materials for autonomous solar modulation windows.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Agapanthus africanus (L.) Hoffmann leaves-based carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: The recognition of buildings' huge share in the European Union's energy consumption led to a series of energy policies fostering energy efficiency in the building sector [1]. Energy-efficient building envelopes (exterior walls, foundations, roofs, windows, and doors) are mandatory in buildings that use sustainable energy in the smart city concept. Windows are a part of the solution.

Over the last decades, large-area glazing has become increasingly widespread in the building sector. Windows allow natural light influx and enable outside views while protecting the interior from wind and rain, providing some human comfort. However, they do not adapt to changing weather or seasons, and conventional sun protection systems, such as sun blinds, roller blinds, or curtains, often help find visual and thermal comfort. The obvious solution is the worldwide implementation of buildings with dynamic switchable glazing. Dynamic (smart) windows adapt in real-time to sunlight transmittance (daylight and solar heat inflow) at the occupant's wish, independently of the building's orientation and climate region. According to the 2024 Market Research Report, the global smart glass market size reached $5.0 billion in 2022 and is projected to reach $8.2 billion by 2027, with an average growth rate of 10.4 % [2].

A window experiences incident solar radiation comprising ultraviolet (UV), visible (vis), and near-infrared (NIR) radiations. Solar energy from the invisible solar spectrum (UV and NIR spectral regions) encompasses about 60 % of the solar energy, of which 50 % belongs to the NIR region. The trade-off between natural daylight and excessive solar heat gain is efficiently regulated by switchable glazing, especially in hot climates [3–5]. The window should allow vis radiation (daylight) influx because it impacts human mood and well-being, reducing stress and curbing artificial lighting energy demand. Yet, glare should be avoided, as it is a major source of visual discomfort. Windows should also allow NIR radiation to pass through, but in a controlled manner, as it affects the building's heating and cooling consumption. In warmer conditions, they should minimize the heat entering the building. In contrast, they should maximize heat transmission in cooler conditions to help maintain a comfortable indoor temperature.

Over the last decade, cooling has become increasingly important in buildings due to global warming, rising living standards, and the growing heat island effect in cities. This has led to increasing energy consumption and climate change. Radiative cooling (RC) emerged as a renewable and sustainable solution that does not require external energy. RC is a passive technology that uses thermal radiation to dissipate heat from objects through the atmosphere into the cold outer space (3 K) [6,7]. Passive daytime RC materials must satisfy two requirements: (1) in the UV–vis–NIR solar spectral range (0.3–2.5 μm), they must have an extremely low absorptivity to minimize solar heat absorption; (2) in the long-wave infrared (LWIR, usually known as the “atmospheric transparency window”) thermal spectral range (8–13 μm), they must exhibit high emissivity (εLWIR) to maximize heat dispersion into outer space. A few RC materials have been integrated in windows [8,9]. Global warming originating from the energy consumption associated with cooling causes severe damage to the ozone layer, increases the intensity of UV radiation, and affects human health. Carbon dots (CDs) have been proposed as useful UV-shielding candidates [10–12].

Three classes of materials - chromogenic materials, liquid crystals, and electrophoretic/suspended particles - have been made available on the commercial smart window market. Significant research efforts have been devoted to the development of windows based on electro-, thermo-, mechano-, and photo-responsive materials [13–19]. The electrochromic window (ECW) technology has been the most widely explored.

Thermotropic windows (TTWs) offer an appealing alternative to ECWs for architectural glazing [20,21]. Unlike EC materials, which require an electrical stimulus to change their optical properties, TT materials alter their light-scattering properties in response to temperature. The transition from a transparent/clear (OFF) state to a light-scattering/opaque (ON) state occurs when the temperature of the window glass rises above the material's transition temperature (Tt). Thus, TTWs are a zero-energy solution that relies on sunlight for activation. These windows offer self-regulation (no active control for operation), do not need any energy consumption (no electrical power supply), exhibit high solar modulation between the OFF and ON states, require minimal maintenance, are cost-effective, and can be produced in various shapes and sizes with excellent long-term stability and easy installation [22]. The Tt of the TT material should be close to room temperature for optimal window energy efficiency. TTDs are particularly suitable for applications where an unobstructed view, such as skylight, roof windows, and façade elements, is not a concern. Binary polymer blends, hydrogels, casting resins, and thermoplastic films are some of the TT classes of materials that have been explored [23]. Four mechanisms are accountable for the switching phenomenon: phase separation for polymer blends and gels, particle size change for TT nanoparticles, aggregation for block copolymers, and phase transition for casting resins with permanent domains [23,24].

Within the scope of the development of integrated thermo-electro-optical devices, we introduced a new ionanofluid (INF), composed of CDs derived from synthetic glucose (GLU) and benign 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([BMIm]Cl) ionic liquid (IL) [25–27]. The role of this IL in the INF formulation was pivotal, serving both as a reaction medium and a functionalization agent. In addition, it acted as a TT agent, due to a reversible TT transition around 30–40 °C, primarily associated with a trans-to-gauche conformational change of the cation butyl side chain (Fig. 1 b) [28,29]. The integrated self-healing device operated either in TT or EC configuration. At 36 °C, under zero-energy sun-actuated operation, the dual-mode TT device (TTDGLU) exhibited a transmittance variation (ΔT) of 9 % at 550/1000 nm. Because this INF is made of CDs, activation of the surface plasmon resonance effect (SPRE) became possible. The plasmon-induced photothermal effect exhibited by silver (Ag) islands was explored to induce an effective light-to-heat conversion in the INF [30]. The plasmons' excitation and transformation into propagating photons amplified the ΔT to 47/31 %.

Subsequently, we adopted the same strategy to synthesize the active layer of a TTD incorporating a waste biomass-derived INF composed of crab shell chitin (CHI)-based CDs and [BMIm]Cl and 1-tosylate-3-methyl-imidazolium triflate ([Tmi][Trif]) ILs [26]. When the temperature was raised from 16 to 50 °C, the ΔT of the TTDCHI at 550 nm was enhanced from 12.5 to 54.3 % via SPRE [26].

More recently, we reported a sustainable TTD concept featuring a kappa-carrageenan (CARR) matrix doped with CDs derived from the Arundo donax leaf and [BMIm]Cl [31]. At 40 and 60 °C, the TTDCARR operated as a dual-mode system with ΔT values of 10/8 % and 13/10 % at 550/1000 nm, respectively. The SPRE-mediated TTDCARR imparted a three-mode operation featuring a bright hot mode (vis and NIR admitted), a semi-bright warm mode (vis and NIR semi-blocked), and a semi-dark semi-cool mode (vis and NIR further semi-blocked), with ΔT values of 42/40 % and 58/55 % at 40 and 60 °C, respectively, and stable performance over multiple cycles.

In this work, we have decided to develop for the first time TT windows with UV-blocking properties and efficient RC performance for hot environments, for which the following requirements should be fulfilled: low Tsol for alleviating indoor solar heating in the daytime and high εLWIR for facilitating indoor heat dissipation during daytime and nighttime [8,9].

Herein, we explore a new TT layer design inspired by transparent sol-gel-derived electrolytes proposed recently for ECWs based on a di-urea cross-linked poly(oxypropylene) (POP)/siloxane hybrid framework [32]. This hybrid structure, d-U′(Y) (where U′ stands for urea, Y = 2000 is the average molecular weight of the POP chains in g mol−1, corresponding to about 33 OP repeat units), seemed quite attractive. Polymers that possess functional groups absorbing strongly within the 1250-769 cm−1 (8–13 μm) interval of the fingerprint region of the mid-infrared spectra (or LWIR band) are useful to prepare RC materials [7]. This is because these vibration modes endow continuous emission of thermal radiation. Thus, d-U′(Y), a hybrid framework which contains C–O–C groups in the POP repeat units and Si–O–Si groups in the siliceous network, similarly to poly(oxyethylene) (POE) and poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS), is expected to exhibit a high εLWIR value as these two polymers (εLWIR = 0.78 [33] and εLWIR = 0.75–0.95 [34], respectively) [7]. [BMIm]Cl was added to the d-U′(Y) matrix to impart the thermo-responsive effect (TT) sought (Fig. 1a). In addition, we incorporated CDs derived from Agapanthus Africanus (L.) Hoffmann leaves (Fig. 1a) with a dual purpose: (1) to act as a sustainable UV-blocking species; (2) to activate the SPRE and amplify the TT phenomenon. We first addressed the physical-chemical properties of these leaves [35]. Then, we optimized the CDs' synthesis [36]. CDs carbonized (CZ) at 300 °C for 3 h (CZ-300-3) were selected here. The goal of the present work was to evaluate the potential of the d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 (where composition n represents the (OCH(CH3)CH2)/[BMIm]Cl) molar ratio) films for TTD applications and RC (Fig. 1). The devices will henceforth be noted as TTDdU'n with n = 50, 10, and 5. These three concentrations were chosen based on previous studies [32]. The devices incorporating Ag islands will be indicated as Ag-TTDdU'n.
CDs: A. africanus leaves collected from the Botanical Garden of UTAD.

Silver islands’ layer: (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, Sigma-Aldrich, >98 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, Sigma-Aldrich, >99.8 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥98 %), D-glucose (Fisher Scientific) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, Alfa Aesar, 28 %) were used as received. Ethanol (EtOH, Fisher Scientific, 99.8 %) were used as received and high-purity distilled water were used in all experiments.

TT layer: (3-Isocyanatopropyl)triethoxysilane (ICPTES, Acros Organics, 95 %), Jeffamine D-2000 (Huntsman), tetrahydrofuran (THF, Fisher Scientific, 99.9 %), diiodomethane (Thermo Scientific, 99 %), and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([BMIm]Cl, Acros Organics, 98 %). EtOH (Fisher Scientific, 99.8 %) were used as received and high-purity distilled water were used in all experiments.

CDs: The CZ-300-3 CDs were synthesized from A. africanus leaves, following the procedure detailed elsewhere [36]. To produce the CDs, the leaves were carbonized at 300 °C for 3 h. They were then extracted and purified.

Silver islands’ layer: To prepare the Ag islands we slightly modified a protocol reported in the literature [25,37].
Step 1: Surface treatment of glass coverslips (Fig. S1a in the Supplementary Material). A solution containing 20 mL of deionized water, 372 mL of 98 % EtOH, and 8 mL of APTES was prepared for glass surface functionalization. This solution was allowed to pre-hydrolyze for 5 min before the coverslips were immersed for 10 min. After immersion, the coverslips were removed and washed with EtOH, followed by a 5-min EtOH soak. This washing and soaking process was repeated four times. Once the coverslips dried, they were ready for Ag islands deposition.
Step 2: Preparation of the silver precursor solution (Fig. S1b in the Supplementary Material). A solution of 0.5 g of AgNO3 in 60 mL of deionized water was prepared in a beaker. While stirring, 8 drops of a 5 % (w/v) NaOH solution were slowly added, forming a dark brown precipitate. Approximately 2 mL of NH4OH was added drop by drop, under stirring, until the solution became clear again, and the beaker was placed in an ice bath.
Step 3: Silver reduction and deposition (Fig. S1c in the Supplementary Material). Once the solution had cooled, the functionalized coverslips were fixed vertically along the beaker walls using tape. A separate solution of 0.72 g D-glucose in 15 mL deionized water was prepared and added to the beaker. The beaker was allowed to return to room temperature for approximately 20–25 min, during which the solution color changed from transparent to yellowish-brown, indicating the reduction of Ag+ ions and the formation of Ag islands on the glass surface. After this color change, the coverslips were removed from the solution, rinsed with deionized water, and left to dry. The coverslips were then placed in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h to guarantee the complete Ag island deposition.

TT layer: The d-U′(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 hybrid layer was synthesized using the procedure detailed elsewhere [32]. The precursor of the d-U′(2000) hybrids was prepared from the reaction of the terminal amine groups of the POP-functionalized diamine with ICPTES. A volume of 260 μL of ICPTES was added to a solution of THF (10 mL) containing 1.0 g of Jeffamine D-2000 (molar proportion ICPTES: Jeffamine = 2:1) in a fume cupboard. The mixture was stirred for 48 h at room temperature. The progress of the reaction was monitored by Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy by following the disappearance of the very strong and sharp –NCO stretching band at 2273 cm−1 and the concomitant growth of characteristic bands of the urea group in the 1800-1500 cm−1 interval. After that, we pursued the sequential addition of [BMIm]Cl, EtOH, HCl (0.5 M, 12 μL) (molar ratio 0.012:1), and CZ-300-3 CDs were incorporated into the system, under stirring, to trigger the sol formation. In agreement with the terminology adopted in previous publications, the hybrids were identified using the notation d-U’(2000)n[BMIm]Cl_CZ-300-3 (where n represents the molar ratio of oxypropylene units per [BMIm]Cl). Transparent and flexible xerogel films were obtained in less than 24 h, without a drying step in an oven.

Polarized Optical Microscopy (POM). POM images were captured using an OPTIKA B-600POL microscope equipped with an 8 MP digital camera, and the OPTIKAVision Pro software was employed to process and analyze the recorded images.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM). AFM measurements were performed in oscillating mode using a Nano-Observer AFM microscope (CSInstruments, France). For high-resolution imaging, a super-sharp Si HQ:NSC19/ACTA probe (frequency resonance: 260 kHz, spring constant: 0.3 N/m) was used. To enhance the quality of the AFM images, the Gwyddion 2.52 program was utilized, applying tools for flattening, eliminating line noise, and implementing a low-pass filter.

Wettability and Surface Free Energy (SFE). The wettability of the Ag-decorated glass and the hybrid materials was evaluated using static water contact angle measurements, and the surface free energy (SFE) analysis was performed exclusively on the hybrid material. The static contact angle measurements were conducted using a Krüss DSA25S drop-shape analyzer, controlled via ADVANCE software, in a temperature-controlled chamber (25 ± 1 °C). The sessile drop technique was employed, where liquid droplets were carefully deposited onto the surface of the Ag-decorated glass and hybrid material. The volume of the distilled water and diiodomethane droplets was kept constant at 5 μL. Contact angles were determined using the Young-Laplace fitting method, with digital images captured by a high-resolution video camera. Measurements were conducted at distinct locations on each sample, with ten measurements per location, ensuring accuracy and reproducibility. The reported values represent the arithmetic mean of all measurements, with data reliability assessed using root mean square error analysis.

The SFE of the hybrid materials was determined using the Owens, Wendt, Rabel, and Kaelble (OWRK) method [38], expressed in Equation (1):
(1)
γ
L V
(
1 + cos
θ
)
= 2
(
γ
L V
D
γ
S V
D
+
γ
L V
P
γ
S V
P
)
)
γ
L V
: Surface tension at the liquid-vapor interface;
θ
: Contact angle;
γ
L V
D
: Dispersive component of surface tension at the liquid-vapor interface;
γ
S V
D
: Dispersive component of surface tension at the solid-vapor interface;
γ
L V
P
: Polar component of surface tension at the liquid-vapor interface;
γ
S V
P
: Polar component of surface tension at the solid-vapor interface;

The OWRK method is based on contact angle measurements using at least two test liquids—one predominantly polar (usually water) and one predominantly dispersive (usually diiodomethane) — both with well-characterized
γ
L V
D
and
γ
S V
P
values. This method relies on the following assumptions: the solvent used is pure, the solid surface is chemically homogeneous and there are no chemical reactions between the solvent and the solid surface. Using this approach, the wettability of the hybrid material is not determined solely by its total SFE, but rather by the interaction between its polar and dispersive components with the test liquids. Based on the calculated SFE values, wettability envelopes of the hybrid surfaces were generated using a spreadsheet provided by Nanoscience Instruments [39].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC). TGA and DSC measurements were conducted using a Netzsch STA 449 F3 Jupiter analyzer, operated with Proteus software. Sample pieces (4–6 mg) were placed in an alumina crucible and analyzed under a high-purity nitrogen atmosphere. The samples were heated from room temperature to 700 °C at a constant rate of 10 °C min−1, with gas flow rates set at 50 mL min−1 for purge and 20 mL min−1 for protection.

Ultraviolet–visible-Near-Infrared (UV–vis–NIR) Spectroscopy. Optical transmittance spectra were recorded in the 400–1000 nm range using a Cary 50 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer, and in the NIR range (1000–1650 nm) with an Ocean Optics DH Mini NIR light source paired with a deuterium lamp. FT-IR spectra were obtained at room temperature using an IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer coupled with attenuated total reflectance (ATR) featuring a diamond ATR crystal. The samples were placed on the crystal, and the spectrum was recorded in the 4000–400 cm−1 range by averaging 128 scans at a resolution of 2 cm−1.

Infrared thermal imaging. The thermal response of the TTDdU’ was assessed using infrared thermal imaging, conducted with a Fluke TiS75 thermal imager, and the collected data were analyzed using SmartView Classic software (version 4.4). Cycling tests were carried out to evaluate the stability and durability of the device over multiple heating and cooling cycles.

X-ray diffraction (XRD). Diffractograms were performed to analyze the structural properties of the hybrid materials. The diffractograms were recorded in the 2θ range of 10–60° at room temperature using a Philips X'Pert MPD Powder detector with monochromated Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.541 Å) and a resolution of 0.02°. The samples were analyzed in film form and were not subjected to any thermal pre-treatment before measurement.

The configuration of the Ag-TTDdU'n prototypes and the working principle of the SPRE are schematically shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. Details regarding the characterization of the Ag-decorated islands may be found in the Supplementary Material (Figs. S2, S3, and S4, respectively).

The TGA curves of the three samples are very similar, revealing essentially three distinct degradation stages, one associated with water and solvent loss, and the other two with the decomposition of [BMIm]Cl (reported between 246 and 300 °C [28]) and the hybrid matrix (Fig. 2 a). Below 150 °C, the sample with n = 5 loses 5 % of the mass, whereas the less concentrated samples lose only 3 %. Between 250 and 300 °C, while 10 % of the mass of the [BMIm]Cl-rich material is lost, the other two samples lose just 3 %. Beyond 330 °C all samples undergo major decomposition, with final residues of 11 % (n = 5), 7 % (n = 10), and 6 % (n = 50) at 700 °C. These results suggest that a higher [BMIm]Cl content slightly impacted the thermal stability. In the DSC curves, the exothermic peaks observed correspond to the matrix decomposition (Fig. 2b).

The XRD patterns reproduced in Fig. 2c confirm the amorphous nature of the hybrids, which produce a characteristic broad siliceous band centered around 21° [40]. Its second order is seen at about 42° (Fig. 2c). The origin of the weak peak observed at 29° for n = 50, which persists in the n = 10 hybrid but disappears in the sample with n = 5, is unknown at this stage. In contrast, in the case of the most concentrated sample, the disappearance of the peak at 29° and the emergence of a prominent peak around 22° induce us to suggest the occurrence of structural rearrangements in the POP domains. They might be associated with enhanced ordering within the hybrid matrix, potentially due to the large IL content facilitating better alignment of the POP chains.

From the three-dimensional (3D) topographical AFM images of the surface morphology of the hybrids shown in Fig. 3 a–c, Ra values of 48.23 nm (n = 50), 74.86 nm (n = 10), and 86.72 nm (n = 5) were calculated. The increasing roughness correlates with the higher concentration of [BMIm]Cl, suggesting that the IL promoted surface structuring.

The contact angle measurements revealed the samples' hydrophilic nature. Yet, as the [BMIm]Cl concentration increased, the surface became less hydrophilic (Fig. 4 a). The n = 5 hybrid exhibited the highest contact angle values and, thus, lower wettability. Accordingly, the total SFE values of the hybrids denoted a slight decrease with the increase in IL concentration (65.63, 63.26, and 61.33 mNm−1, respectively) (Fig. 4b).

The wettability envelopes provide valuable insights into the interfacial properties of the hybrid materials, illustrating their potential interaction with polar and dispersive liquids. The hybrid with n = 50 displayed the largest polar component (Fig. 4b), leading to stronger interactions with highly polar solvents, such as water and glycerol (Fig. 5 a). This result correlates with its higher SFE and lower contact angle, indicating greater hydrophilicity. The hybrid with n = 5, in contrast, exhibited the largest dispersive component ( Fig. 4b), thus demonstrating less interaction with such solvents (Fig. 5c). The sample with n = 10 represents an intermediate case (Fig. 5b), balancing polar and dispersive contributions (Fig. 4b).
Fig. 6 a–c reproduce the UV–vis–NIR spectra of the Ag-TTDdU'n with n ranging from n = 50 to 5 in the 30–70 °C range.
These findings show that the progressive increase of [BMIm]Cl concentration in the active layer benefited TTD operation.

In the case of the dilute layer with n = 50, the ΔT values at 70 °C were practically coincident at 555 and 1100 nm (6 and 5 %, respectively) (red line, Fig. 6a and g, and Table S1 in the Supplementary Material), indicating poor TT performance. For n = 10, there was a three-fold increase of these ΔT values (19 and 18 %, respectively) (red line, Fig. 6b and g, and Table S1 in the Supplementary Material). At the highest [BMIm]Cl content (n = 5), the ΔT values further reached 31 and 27 % (red line, Fig. 6c and g, and Table S1 in the Supplementary Material), respectively, thus ca. 5 times higher than those observed at n = 50, denoting a substantial enhancement in the layer's ability to modulate light and heat in response to temperature changes.

This trend indicates that the progressive incorporation of [BMIm]Cl led to a more important TT phenomenon. The clear increase in cloudiness with the temperature rise, particularly evident at the highest [BMIm]Cl concentrations, was captured with a visible camera (Fig. 6d–f) and a thermal camera (Fig. 7 d–f).

Comparing the performance of TTDs incorporating different materials may be rather speculative, as it depends on the climate region of the building where it will be implemented. Anyway, it is of interest to compare the performance of TTDdU’5 versus that of the TTDCARR reported by Nunes et al., [31] which featured remarkable ΔT values at 40 and 60 °C (42/40 % and 58/55 % at 550/1200 nm respectively). Despite the lower ΔT values, TTDdU’5 stands out for its broad operational range (30–70 °C) and noteworthy cycling stability, making it suitable for hot climate regions and long-term use.

Fig. 7a–c illustrate the results of the cycling stability tests of the three Ag-TTDdU'n devices at 30 and 70 °C. For n = 50, the transmittance at room temperature remained relatively stable over 10 cycles, whereas at 70 °C there was a slight decline, indicating acceptable cycling stability despite the very low ΔT of 6 % (Fig. 7a). For n = 10, the stability over 10 cycles remained practically constant (Fig. 7b), indicating that the intermediate concentration of [BMIm]Cl tested provided a good ΔT and durability balance. At n = 5 (Fig. 7c), the transmittance at room temperature remained nearly the same over the 10 cycles, whereas that at 70 °C demonstrated a progressive decrease. This implies that the performance of the device improved with cycling. During the first three cycles, a ΔT of 15–20 % resulted, and this difference increased to 35–40 % in subsequent cycles. This finding can be tentatively attributed to the gradual stabilization of the material's TT properties after initial activation, probably associated with a progressive structural adjustment process.

The Ag-TTDdU’5 was further tested by performing an additional batch of 10 heating/cooling cycles after leaving the device at rest for 1 month. Fig. 7c reveals a ΔT of approximately 33 %, thus of the same order of magnitude as that measured earlier.

Fig. 8 illustrates the performance of Ag-TTDdU′5 in the solar region (0.4–1.3 μm) and shows the transmittance spectrum of d-U′(2000)5[BMIm]Cl in the thermal region (3–20 μm). The sharp minimum centered at ca. 9.2 μm (1086 cm−1) is due to the stretching vibration mode of the C–O–C bonds of the polymer chains. This feature implies a corresponding absorptance maximum, and, consequently, an emittance peak is expected in this region of the atmospheric transparency window.

In this study, we demonstrate the potential of using a di-urea cross-linked POP/siloxane hybrid matrix, doped with UV-shielding A. africanus leaves-derived CDs and variable concentrations of TT [BMIm]Cl, as the active layer of an Ag-decorated TTD for synchronous vis/NIR control smart windows with RC ability. The combination of the SPRE effect provided by Ag islands, with the UV absorption of the CDs and the TT properties imparted by [BMIm]Cl over the vis-NIR region, contributes to the overall enhanced performance of the TTDs. The increase of [BMIm]Cl concentration boosted the TT performance of the device. The highest transmittance modulation values at 550/1100 nm were 31/27 %. The cycling stability of this device was confirmed for 10 heating/cooling cycles after device assembly and 10 consecutive heating/cooling cycles after a 1-month rest.

The next stage of this work will be to perform accelerated durability tests in an environmental weathering camera to simulate severe weather conditions using UV light, extreme temperatures, humidity, and flooding. Subsequently, the thermal performance of up-scaled devices under real-climate conditions will be tested and compared with conventional window solutions.
The sustainable and eco-friendly materials-oriented approach employed in the Ag-TTDdU’5 design concept may open exciting new avenues in RC solar-powered windows.

Paulo Joaquim Nunes: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mariana Fernandes: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Verónica de Zea Bermudez: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The work was developed under project A-MoVeR – “Mobilizing Agenda for the Development of Products; Systems towards an Intelligent and Green Mobility”, operation 02/C05-i01.01/2022.
